title: Pressures: Soaring costs will bring double-digit premium rises
full_text:
The country's biggest insurers say the rising cost of natural disasters and inflation in the construction industry will push premiums up by double digits every year for the foreseeable future, setting up a showdown with consumers and regulators.
The warnings come as the Albanese government demands insurers improve how they explain the reasons for premium rises - including the drivers of the increases - and with the Australian Securities and Investments Commission preparing to examine a code of conduct the industry abides by.
IAG chief executive Nick Hawkins, whose company runs NRMA Insurance and CGU, said intense cost pressures on construction had exposed home insurance to "double-digit [premium growth] for some time".
That would not ease, Hawkins told The Australian Financial Review Insurance Summit in Sydney on Tuesday, until "our country can really address some of these property inflationary pressures".
Persistent increases in construction costs worsened with the war between the United States and Iran this year, which led to a spike in prices for materials. In April, Barrenjoey warned that the cost of new houses and renovations would be running at close to 6 per cent by September.
The Australian Climate Service has separately suggested that the annual cost of natural disasters - including floods, bushfires and tropical cyclones - could reach $40 billion by 2050. This is nearly 10 times the average annual insured loss of $4.5 billion recorded between 2019 and 2024, which was itself 67 per cent higher than the preceding five years.
Lisa Harrison, the consumer insurance chief at Suncorp, said lengthy delays in building approvals and skilled labour shortages were also contributing to the rebuilding of homes becoming more costly.
Sue Houghton, the chief executive of QBE Insurance's Australian business, said regulations added around $200,000 to building a home in a major city.
Those concerns, raised by two of the country's largest insurers, come as the regulator says it won't sign off on a reworked industry code of conduct unless it addresses recommendations made by a parliamentary inquiry two years ago. The inquiry examined shortcomings in how insurance companies dealt with customers after the devastating 2022 floods.
"When we ultimately come to make a decision on approval, the degree to which there is a significant divergence between the views of the industry and the views of key stakeholders will be a factor that [we] will take into account," ASIC commissioner Alan Kirkland said. "Then the other key factor will be looking at the degree to which the revised code has responded to the ... recommendations that have been made."
The Albanese government is open to bolstering infrastructure funding and tightening development standards through the twin policies of the Disaster Ready Fund and the Hazards Insurance Partnership. The $1 billion Disaster Ready Fund was allocated over five years to mid-2028, but is well below the $30 billion over a decade the industry has said needs to be spent on creating stronger defences against floods, which will become worse as sea levels rise.
While insurers said better transparency would bolster their licence to operate, they warned it was crucial to not increase regulations.
Financial Services Minister Daniel Mulino said the insurance sector was a challenging market, suggesting a "traffic light system" could identify major hazard risk for each policy to explain to customers why prices rose.
"We as government, as regulators, as industry, as consumer groups, we're all going to need to work together to figure out a way in which we can do a better job of providing a layered understanding for policyholders of what it is that's driving the level of their policies," Mulino said.
State governments, meanwhile, are focusing on land use and planning to limit risk despite pressures to build more housing supply.
"We have a housing challenge, but that doesn't mean we build in the highest risk areas for the sake of actually getting our numbers up," said NSW Building Minister Anoulack Chanthivong. The state, he added, was "mindful of the cost pressures to the building industry ... but that doesn't mean we actually then build homes that are not obviously adapting to the climate".
Fairfax Media Management Pty Limited
author: James Eyers and Joyce Moullakis
heading: General Insurance
